exercises.title: Image to patch tokens
exercises.prompt: Explain the ViT patch-token pipeline: how an H×W×3 image becomes a sequence of tokens (patchify, linear projection, position embedding), and why this 'patch-token primitive' is the foundation every 2026 multimodal model still builds on.
exercises.hint: Split into fixed patches (e.g. 16×16), flatten + linearly project each to d_model, add positional encoding → a token sequence a transformer can consume. The primitive held; only the encoder/positions/registers changed.
exercises.solution:
**The pipeline:**
1. **Patchify:** split the `H×W×3` image into a grid of **fixed-size patches** (e.g. 16×16 pixels). For 224×224 with 16×16 patches → `(224/16)² = 196` patches.
2. **Linear projection:** flatten each patch (16×16×3 = 768 values) and pass it through **one learned linear layer** to a `d_model`-dim **patch embedding**.
3. **Position embedding:** add a positional encoding to each patch embedding (originally a learned table; in 2026, **2D-RoPE**) so the order-blind transformer knows *where* each patch sits in the grid.
4. Optionally prepend a **[CLS] token** (or use **register tokens** / mean pooling) for an image-level representation.
The output is a **sequence of tokens** — exactly what a transformer consumes, identical in shape to text tokens.

**Why this primitive is the foundation:** the deep insight (Phase 7.09, 9.09) is that **a transformer doesn't care what the tokens *mean*** — feed it a sequence of vectors and it learns relationships. Turning an image into patch tokens makes **vision just another sequence**, so the same transformer machinery, training tricks, and scaling laws apply. This is why **every 2026 frontier multimodal model** (Claude Opus, Gemini, Qwen-Omni) still begins by **patchifying images into tokens** — the primitive held even as the pieces evolved: the **encoder** went ViT → DINOv2 → SigLIP 2, **register tokens** were added (to soak up global info and fix attention artifacts), and the **positional scheme** became **2D-RoPE** (native-resolution-friendly, relative). 'Visual tokens' is the shared currency that lets images flow into the same transformer that processes text — the bedrock of the entire phase. Everything downstream (CLIP alignment, projectors, early-fusion) operates on these patch tokens.

exercises.title: ViT geometry: sequence length, params, FLOPs
exercises.prompt: Implement the ViT geometry calculator: given (patch_size, resolution, hidden_dim, depth), compute the number of patch tokens, the patch-embedding parameter count, and the attention cost. Explain why halving patch size quadruples the token count (and the attention cost) and what that means for high-resolution images.
exercises.hint: tokens = (H/p)·(W/p); patch-embed params = (p²·3)·d; attention is O(tokens²·d). Halve p → 4× tokens → 16× attention. High-res images explode the sequence — the central cost problem of VLMs.
exercises.solution:
```python
def vit_geometry(patch_size, resolution, hidden_dim, depth):
    H = W = resolution
    tokens = (H // patch_size) * (W // patch_size)           # patch count
    patch_embed_params = (patch_size * patch_size * 3) * hidden_dim  # linear proj
    # per-layer attention cost ~ O(tokens^2 * d); FFN ~ O(tokens * d^2)
    attn_flops = depth * (tokens * tokens * hidden_dim)
    ffn_flops  = depth * (tokens * hidden_dim * hidden_dim * 4)
    return {"tokens": tokens, "patch_embed_params": patch_embed_params,
            "attn_flops": attn_flops, "ffn_flops": ffn_flops}

for p in (16, 8):
    g = vit_geometry(p, 224, 768, 12)
    print(f"patch={p}: {g['tokens']} tokens, attn_flops={g['attn_flops']:.2e}")
```

**The geometry:**
- **Token count** = `(H/p) × (W/p)` — the grid of patches. For 224px, p=16 → 196 tokens; p=8 → 784 tokens.
- **Patch-embedding params** = `(p² × 3) × d` — the linear projection from flattened-patch to embedding. Larger patches = more params per patch (but fewer patches).
- **Attention cost** ≈ `O(tokens² × d)` per layer — quadratic in token count.

**Why halving patch size quadruples tokens (and 16×s attention):** token count is `(H/p)·(W/p)` — **inversely proportional to p²**. Halve `p` (16→8) and each spatial dimension doubles its patch count, so total tokens **×4** (196→784). Since **attention is O(tokens²)**, 4× tokens means **16× attention cost**. Smaller patches give **finer spatial detail** (better OCR, small objects) but at **quadratically exploding cost**.

**What this means for high-resolution images:** resolution and patch size *both* drive the token count, and **tokens are the VLM's central cost/budget constraint** (the visual-token budget that recurs throughout Phase 12). A 2576px image (Claude Opus native) at p=16 is `(2576/16)² ≈ 25,900` tokens *per image* — enormous, and attention is quadratic in that. So high-res vision forces hard tradeoffs: **larger patches (cheaper, less detail), pooling/compression (fewer tokens), or smarter packing** (any-resolution, Lesson 6). This token-budget pressure is *why* the rest of the phase obsesses over connectors, pooling, AnyRes tiling, and token compression — the patch-token primitive is simple, but its **quadratic cost in resolution** is the problem every VLM architecture is fighting.

exercises.title: The three upgrades from 2020 ViT to 2026 production, and pooling choices
exercises.prompt: Name the three upgrades that took ViT from 2020 research to 2026 production (self-supervised pretraining, register tokens, native-resolution packing) and what each fixed. Then explain when to use CLS pooling vs mean pooling vs register tokens for a downstream task, and why the vision encoder choice matters more than the connector (foreshadowing the recipe lesson).
exercises.hint: Self-supervised (DINO/MAE/SigLIP) → better features without labels. Register tokens → fix attention artifacts / global info sink. Native-res packing → handle any aspect ratio. CLS=single global vector, mean=robust pooled, registers=clean global + per-patch. Encoder>connector per ablations.
exercises.solution:
**The three upgrades (2020 ViT → 2026 production):**
1. **Self-supervised pretraining (DINO / MAE / SigLIP).** The original ViT needed huge **labeled** datasets (JFT-300M) to beat CNNs. **Self-supervised methods** — DINO (self-distillation), MAE (masked autoencoding), and contrastive (CLIP/SigLIP, Lesson 2) — learn rich visual features from **unlabeled or weakly-labeled web data**, producing far better, more transferable representations cheaply. This is what made strong vision towers available off-the-shelf.
2. **Register tokens.** Researchers found ViT attention maps had **artifacts** — high-norm 'outlier' tokens where the model dumped global information, corrupting patch features. Adding a few **register tokens** (extra learnable tokens with no patch content) gives the model **dedicated scratch space** for global info, **cleaning up the per-patch features** and improving dense tasks. (DINOv2 with registers.)
3. **Native-resolution packing.** Fixed 224² square resize **destroys aspect ratio and detail** (Lesson 6). Native-resolution methods (NaViT patch-n'-pack, M-RoPE, NaFlex) let one model **process any aspect ratio / resolution** without distortion — essential for OCR, documents, charts, screenshots.

**Pooling choices for downstream tasks:**
- **CLS pooling:** use the **[CLS] token's** final representation as the single image vector. Good for **image-level classification/retrieval** where you want one global summary; it's a learned aggregator. Simple, but can be dominated by the artifact problem (hence registers).
- **Mean pooling:** average all patch tokens. **Robust and simple** — often beats CLS for retrieval/embedding when no CLS was trained, and is less sensitive to any single token. Good default for **whole-image embeddings**.
- **Register tokens (+ patch tokens):** use **clean per-patch features** (for **dense/spatial tasks** — detection, segmentation, grounding, OCR where *where* matters) with register tokens absorbing global info. When your downstream task needs **spatial/per-region** information (most VLM tasks feed *all* patch tokens to the LLM), you want the **full patch sequence with clean features**, which registers provide.
Rule: **single global vector needed → CLS or mean pooling; spatial/dense task or feeding an LLM → all patch tokens (with registers for clean features).**

**Why encoder choice > connector (foreshadowing Lesson 7):** the controlled ablations across MM1, Cambrian-1, Idefics2, Prismatic (Lesson 7) converge on a clear result: **the vision encoder matters more than the connector architecture.** Intuitively, the encoder determines **what visual information is even available** — a SigLIP 2 vs an old CLIP tower changes the *quality and richness* of the patch tokens, which bounds everything downstream. The connector (MLP vs Q-Former vs cross-attention) just **routes** those features into the LLM; if the features are good, even a simple 2-layer MLP works (LLaVA's lesson, Lesson 5). So the highest-leverage choice is **which encoder produces your patch tokens** (and at what resolution), not how fancy the bridge is — and **data mixture matters more than either** (Lesson 7). The patch-token primitive is fixed; the *quality* of those tokens (encoder + resolution + clean features via registers) is what you optimize. This is why the phase opens with the encoder and the patch-token geometry — they're the foundation that determines the ceiling for every multimodal model built on top.
